content.text: The Communication Contract Between Researcher and Organisation

When a security researcher reports a vulnerability, they are entering an implicit trust relationship. They have withheld a potentially valuable finding — one they could sell or publish unilaterally — in exchange for a commitment from you: that you will take the report seriously, respond promptly, remediate honestly, and coordinate disclosure fairly. A researcher coordination process is the operational infrastructure that keeps that commitment.

The most common cause of VDP breakdown is not malicious intent — it is silence. A researcher submits a detailed report and receives no substantive response for two weeks. They assume it was lost or ignored, and they publish. A good coordination process eliminates silence as a possible outcome at every stage.

Good researcher coordination is not just a courtesy — it is a security control. Researchers who have a positive coordination experience become repeat contributors. Those who have a poor experience publish immediately or share findings in private communities where you have no visibility.
Swimlane Overview: Roles and Handoffs

attrs.alt: Five-lane swimlane showing researcher coordination flow: Submission → Intake Bot → Triage Lead → Engineering → CISO/Legal, including out-of-scope and patch/disclosure paths

content.text: Stage 1: Initial Acknowledgement (Target: Within 1 Hour)

The first response to a submitted vulnerability report should be automated — a ticket confirmation with an ID, an estimated triage timeline, and a brief summary of what the researcher can expect next. This is not a substantive response; it is proof of receipt. Its purpose is to reset the researcher's clock and prevent them from assuming the report was lost.
Automated Acknowledgement Template

Subject: [VDP-{ticket_id}] We received your security report

Thank you for submitting a security report through our Vulnerability Disclosure
Programme.

Ticket ID: VDP-{ticket_id}
Received: {timestamp_utc}
Component reported: {component}

We will review your submission and provide an initial scope and severity
assessment within 5 business days. For critical vulnerabilities involving
active exploitation, we aim to respond within 24 hours — if you believe your
submission falls into this category, reply to this message with "URGENT" in
the subject line.

What happens next:
1. Our security triage team will assess scope and reproducibility.
2. We will confirm whether the report falls within the VDP scope.
3. If in scope, we will assign a severity rating and open a remediation ticket.
4. We will keep you informed at least weekly until the issue is resolved.

You can check the status of your submission at any time by replying to this
message.

We do not share submission details with third parties without your consent,
except as required by applicable law.

— [Organisation] Security Team
Stage 2: Triage and Scope Determination (Target: Within 5 Business Days)

Triage is the process of determining whether a report is in scope, reproducible, and a genuine security concern — and if so, how severe. It is the most cognitively demanding part of the process and the one most likely to be the bottleneck at high submission volume.
Triage Checklist
Check
Pass Condition
Failure Action
Report completeness
Steps to reproduce are clear; impact is stated; affected component is identified
Request clarification within 48h; do not leave incomplete reports in triage queue
Scope match
Affected component appears in the VDP in-scope list
Send OOS notification with explanation; offer to forward to Anthropic if the finding is against Anthropic's infrastructure
Reproducibility
Triage engineer can reproduce the issue using the described steps
Flag for researcher follow-up; ask for additional detail or proof-of-concept
Deduplication
No existing open ticket with the same root cause on the same component
Send duplicate notice; credit the earlier reporter; notify the duplicate submitter of the existing fix timeline
Severity assessment
CVSS score or AI-specific severity rubric applied
Document the rationale; escalate if Critical/High requires CISO notification
Legal / PR risk
No indicators that the researcher is hostile, has already disclosed, or is using the finding as leverage
Escalate to legal before any further communication
AI-Specific Severity Rubric

Standard CVSS scoring was designed for conventional software vulnerabilities. It does not capture the multi-step nature of prompt injection chains or the probabilistic nature of model behaviour. The following rubric supplements CVSS for Claude-specific findings:
Severity
Conditions
Examples
Critical
Unauthenticated; leads to system prompt exfiltration, arbitrary code execution via tool calls, or PII exfiltration at scale; fully reproducible
Indirect injection via RAG that reliably executes file-delete tool with no user confirmation
High
Authenticated; leads to significant privilege escalation, user PII exposure for a single session, or reliable jailbreak of restricted deployment
Crafted user message bypasses content filter and extracts CBRN-relevant content from a restricted medical deployment
Medium
Authenticated; probabilistic (works < 50% of the time); limited data exposure; or requires unusual user interaction
Multi-turn prompt that sometimes causes Claude to ignore system prompt instructions; requires specific phrasing
Low
Theoretical; no demonstrated impact; or already mitigated by defence-in-depth controls
Researcher demonstrates that with 1000 attempts, 3 resulted in partial system prompt disclosure
Informational
Behaviour that is unexpected but not exploitable; or best-practice recommendation without demonstrated vulnerability
System prompt is verbose and could be inferred from output patterns; no direct disclosure
Stage 3: Ongoing Communication During Remediation

Once a report is in scope and triaged, the researcher enters a waiting period while your engineering team patches the issue. This period is where most coordination failures occur. The researcher has no visibility into your internal progress; from their perspective, silence means inaction.
Communication Cadence Commitments
Severity
Initial Response SLA
Update Frequency During Patch
Maximum Silence Period
Critical
24 hours
Daily until patch deployed
48 hours
High
5 business days
Weekly
10 business days
Medium
5 business days
Every 2 weeks
30 days
Low
10 business days
Monthly
60 days
Informational
15 business days
Single response with resolution or rationale
30 days
Substantive Update Template
Handling Difficult Coordination Scenarios
The Researcher Threatens to Disclose Early
Do not threaten legal action in return.

Even if you believe the researcher is acting in bad faith, a legal threat issued before they have disclosed accelerates the problem — it confirms to the researcher that you are not a trustworthy coordination partner and may trigger immediate disclosure as a defensive move. Instead:
Acknowledge the researcher's frustration directly and without condescension.
Provide a concrete patch date — not a range, a specific date.
Offer a brief technical call to walk through the remediation plan.
If the researcher insists on disclosure before the patch, agree on the timing and negotiate for coordinated disclosure of the advisory simultaneously.
Document the communication in your legal file in case the researcher discloses prematurely and you need to demonstrate good-faith coordination.
The Report Contains Data Beyond What Was Needed

A researcher who accessed or exfiltrated more data than was necessary to demonstrate the vulnerability is in breach of the VDP safe harbour conditions. The response depends on the severity and apparent intent:
Apparent good faith (excess data incidental to proof of concept).
Remind the researcher of the safe harbour data minimisation requirement; request deletion of any excess data; document the reminder; continue coordination.
Significant data exfiltration with unclear intent.
Pause coordination; escalate to legal and CISO before any further communication; assess regulatory notification obligations under GDPR, CCPA, or applicable breach notification law.
Apparent malicious intent or active exploitation.
Transfer to incident response, not VDP coordination; consult legal before any further response to the 'researcher'.
Duplicate Reports From Multiple Researchers

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def generate_duplicate_notice(original_ticket: str, duplicate_submission: dict) -> str:
    """Generate a respectful duplicate notice for a secondary reporter."""
    prompt = f"""You are a VDP coordinator. Write a professional, respectful message to a security researcher
informing them that the vulnerability they reported (described below) is a duplicate of an existing
ticket ({original_ticket}) that was reported earlier and is currently being remediated.

Their submission:
Title: {duplicate_submission["title"]}
Severity: {duplicate_submission["severity"]}
Component: {duplicate_submission["component"]}

The message should:
1. Thank them genuinely — finding the same vulnerability independently demonstrates it is a real issue
2. Confirm we already have a fix in progress (without disclosing the other researcher's identity or details)
3. State that we will note their independent discovery in our records and, with their permission, include
   them in the hall of fame acknowledgement alongside the original reporter
4. Provide the expected disclosure date so they know when they can publish their own write-up
5. Be under 200 words

Tone: warm, professional, respectful of the researcher's effort."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text


dup = {
    "title": "System prompt exfiltration via indirect injection in document uploader",
    "severity": "High",
    "component": "/api/v1/documents/analyze"
}
print(generate_duplicate_notice("VDP-2024-047", dup))
Stage 4: Coordinating Disclosure

When the patch is deployed, the coordination process shifts from remediation to disclosure. The researcher has been waiting — sometimes for weeks or months — and has their own publication interests. Coordinated disclosure means you agree on what each party will say and when.
Disclosure Negotiation Points
Disclosure date.
Agree on a specific date, not a range. If you need more time for downstream customers to patch, explain why and propose a date; do not simply extend silently.
Content boundaries.

The researcher may publish a technical write-up; you will publish a security advisory. Neither party should disclose information the other considers sensitive — for example, you would not disclose the researcher's name without their consent; they would not publish exploitation code before the patch is widely deployed.
CVE assignment.

If a CVE is appropriate, decide who will request it. For vulnerabilities in your own software, you should request it. For vulnerabilities in a third-party component you use, the CVE may already exist or should be requested by the component maintainer.
Hall of fame or bounty.
Confirm the reward (recognition, payment, or both) before disclosure, so the researcher's write-up can reference it and you have fulfilled your commitment before the issue becomes public.
Embargo.

If the vulnerability has broad industry impact, coordinating disclosure with other affected vendors through CERT/CC or a similar body may be appropriate. This adds complexity; only pursue it for vulnerabilities with genuine industry-wide scope.
Building a Researcher Relationship Programme

Repeat researchers — those who submit multiple validated findings over time — are among the most valuable contributors to your security programme. They have invested time in understanding your architecture and attack surface. Treating them as one-off contacts rather than long-term partners is a significant missed opportunity.
Relationship Tier
Criteria
Benefits
First-time reporter
Single validated finding
Public acknowledgement; VDP-standard response SLA
Returning researcher
Two or more validated findings
Dedicated triage contact; faster acknowledgement SLA (4h); early access to scope expansions
VDP partner
Five or more validated findings, positive coordination history
Private scope previews; direct engineering team access for complex reproductions; advisory co-authorship credit

A researcher relationship programme requires minimal investment — primarily a segmented email list and a designated point of contact for returning researchers. The return is a steady pipeline of informed, high-quality reports from researchers who already understand your architecture.
Tooling and Platform Considerations

The coordination process described above is achievable with email alone at low submission volume — but it becomes unmanageable without tooling once submissions reach 20+ per month. Purpose-built VDP platforms provide structure, but they are not the only option. The right choice depends on your organisation's size, existing tooling, and compliance requirements.
Platform Options Comparison
Option
Best For
Strengths
Limitations
Dedicated email alias (security@)
< 5 submissions/month; early-stage programme
Zero cost; no vendor dependency; researcher-friendly
No SLA tracking; no deduplication; no audit trail without manual logging
HackerOne / Bugcrowd (managed)
Programmes with budget; high researcher volume expected
Researcher community access; built-in triage support; platform manages payouts
10–20% platform fee on bounties; vendor lock-in; researchers may submit low-quality reports for bounty
Intigriti / YesWeHack (European)
EU-regulated organisations; GDPR data residency requirements
EU data residency; GDPR-native design; strong EU researcher community
Smaller researcher pool than US platforms; less mature automation
Self-hosted (JIRA + Vault)
Large enterprises with existing ITSM infrastructure
Full control; integrates with existing workflows; no per-finding fees
No researcher portal; manual outbound communication; requires engineering maintenance
GitHub Security Advisories
Open-source components in your Claude app stack
Free; CVE request integration; familiar to OSS researchers
Not suitable for proprietary systems; public by design after disclosure
Integrating Triage with Your Existing Security Stack

import anthropic, json
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class TriageTicket:
    ticket_id: str
    title: str
    component: str
    severity: str
    researcher_email: str
    steps_to_reproduce: str
    impact: str
    status: str = "pending_triage"
    assignee: str = ""
    jira_key: str = ""
    notes: str = ""

def enrich_triage_ticket(ticket: TriageTicket) -> TriageTicket:
    """
    AI-assisted enrichment: adds severity rationale, JIRA priority,
    and suggested assignee based on component and vulnerability class.
    """
    prompt = f"""You are a security triage coordinator for a Claude-powered enterprise application.
Enrich this VDP triage ticket and respond with a JSON object.

Ticket:
{json.dumps(asdict(ticket), indent=2)}

Respond with JSON containing:
- "severity_rationale": why this severity rating is appropriate (1-2 sentences)
- "jira_priority": "Blocker" | "Critical" | "Major" | "Minor" | "Trivial"
- "suggested_assignee_role": "security-lead" | "ai-safety-team" | "backend-engineering" | "platform-engineering" | "legal"
- "remediation_hints": list of 2-3 specific technical remediation directions
- "similar_cwe": most relevant CWE ID as string (e.g. "CWE-1336" for prompt injection)
- "requires_legal_review": true if report involves data exfiltration or regulatory notification risk"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=768,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    enrichment = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    ticket.notes = (
        f"Severity rationale: {enrichment['severity_rationale']}\n"
        f"CWE: {enrichment['similar_cwe']}\n"
        f"Hints: {'; '.join(enrichment['remediation_hints'])}"
    )
    ticket.assignee = enrichment["suggested_assignee_role"]
    return ticket


ticket = TriageTicket(
    ticket_id="VDP-2024-089",
    title="Tool-call chain abuse allows arbitrary file read",
    component="/api/v1/assistant/tools/file-reader",
    severity="high",
    researcher_email="researcher@example.com",
    steps_to_reproduce="Craft a user message that convinces Claude to call the file-reader tool on /etc/passwd",
    impact="Unauthenticated file read on the server; potential credential exposure."
)
enriched = enrich_triage_ticket(ticket)
print(json.dumps(asdict(enriched), indent=2))
Metrics and Programme Health
A VDP coordination process should be measured continuously, not just audited annually. The following metrics surface whether your process is functioning or degrading:
Metric
Target
What Degradation Looks Like
Time to first substantive response
≤ 5 business days for 95% of submissions
Rising tail — some submissions waiting > 2 weeks signals triage queue overflow
Researcher satisfaction (post-close survey)
Scores below 3.5 often indicate silent periods or severity disputes without explanation
Disclosure deadline compliance
100% — never miss an agreed disclosure date
Any miss damages researcher trust and may trigger hostile early disclosure
Duplicate rate
< 20% of valid submissions
High duplicate rates suggest a known issue is not being patched promptly
Severity override rate
< 15% of researcher self-assessments overridden by triage
High override rate suggests VDP scope or severity rubric documentation is unclear
Mean time to patch (by severity)
Critical ≤ 1 day; High ≤ 7 days; Medium ≤ 30 days
Slippage indicates engineering capacity constraints or deprioritisation

Review coordination metrics monthly with the security team lead and quarterly with CISO. A VDP that receives high-quality reports but patches slowly is only marginally better than no VDP — researchers notice the gap between acknowledgement and resolution.
Common Coordination Anti-Patterns
Triage by committee.

If every severity decision requires sign-off from three people, your SLAs will slip. Empower a designated triage lead to make severity calls independently, with escalation reserved for Critical findings.
Mixing VDP and customer bug reports.

VDP submissions should flow through a separate channel from customer support tickets. Routing them together dilutes priority and creates confidentiality risks if customer-facing staff see undisclosed vulnerability details.
Closing tickets as 'won't fix' without explanation.
Researchers who receive a 'won't fix' closure with no rationale often assume you are deprioritising for cost reasons, not security reasons. Always explain the risk acceptance decision.
Undisclosed policy changes.

If you narrow the VDP scope mid-cycle, notify researchers with in-progress submissions immediately. A researcher who believed their finding was in scope and receives an out-of-scope notice after weeks of coordination will be — justifiably — frustrated.
Using the VDP as a litigation shield only.

Some organisations establish a VDP purely to have legal cover against researchers, with no genuine intent to remediate. Researchers identify this pattern quickly — low response rates, perpetually 'under investigation' findings, no published advisories. The security community shares this information, and the organisation loses access to future reports.
Summary

Researcher coordination is the process that converts a vulnerability report into a fixed issue without triggering a harmful public disclosure. It requires automated acknowledgement within one hour, substantive triage within five business days, a cadence of updates throughout the remediation period, and a coordinated disclosure process that respects both the researcher's publication interests and your customers' need for patch time. AI-specific triage requires a supplemental severity rubric beyond CVSS, since prompt injection and indirect injection chains have exploitability characteristics that conventional scoring does not capture. Difficult scenarios — early disclosure threats, data over-collection, duplicates — each have a defined response pattern that keeps the coordination on track without escalating to adversarial positions.
